title: Dependency Injection Deep Dive
description: Hilt component hierarchy, scoping, assisted inject, multi-module DI, and testing
lesson:
Why Dependency Injection?

DI means a class receives its dependencies from outside instead of creating them itself. This enables: testability (inject fakes), swappability (debug vs release implementations), and loose coupling.

Hilt Component Hierarchy

Hilt (built on Dagger) defines a hierarchy of components that mirror Android's lifecycle scopes. Each component can access bindings from its parent.

SingletonComponent (@Singleton) │ ├── ActivityRetainedComponent (@ActivityRetainedScoped) │ │ │ └── ViewModelComponent (@ViewModelScoped) │ │ │ └── ActivityComponent (@ActivityScoped) │ │ │ ├── FragmentComponent (@FragmentScoped) │ │ │ └── ViewComponent │ └── ServiceComponent (@ServiceScoped)
Key insight: @ViewModelScoped survives configuration changes (backed by ViewModel). @ActivityScoped does NOT — it's destroyed on rotation. Choose scopes based on the data's lifetime, not convenience.

@Binds vs @Provides

Both tell Hilt how to create an instance, but they serve different purposes:

@Binds@Provides
Maps interface → implementationCreates instances with custom logic
Must be abstract fun in abstract classRegular fun in object or class
No code body — Hilt generates the bindingHas a body that constructs the object
More efficient (less generated code)Required when construction has logic
// @Binds — mapping interface to implementation @Module @InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class) abstract class RepositoryModule { @Binds @Singleton abstract fun bindUserRepo(impl: UserRepositoryImpl): UserRepository } // @Provides — when you need construction logic @Module @InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class) object NetworkModule { @Provides @Singleton fun provideRetrofit(): Retrofit = Retrofit.Builder() .baseUrl("https://api.example.com/") .addConverterFactory(MoshiConverterFactory.create()) .build() }
When MUST you use @Provides? When the class is from a library (you can't annotate it with @Inject), when construction requires a builder pattern, or when you need to configure the instance.

Assisted Injection

When a class needs both DI-provided and runtime parameters (e.g., a ViewModel that needs a userId passed from navigation):

class DetailViewModel @AssistedInject constructor( private val repository: UserRepository, // DI-provided @Assisted private val userId: String // Runtime parameter ) : ViewModel() { ... } @AssistedFactory interface DetailViewModelFactory { fun create(userId: String): DetailViewModel }

Multi-Module DI with @EntryPoint

@EntryPoint provides an escape hatch for injecting into classes that Hilt doesn't manage (ContentProviders, 3rd-party classes, dynamic feature modules):

@EntryPoint @InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class) interface AnalyticsEntryPoint { fun getAnalytics(): AnalyticsService } // Usage from a non-Hilt class: val entryPoint = EntryPointAccessors.fromApplication( context, AnalyticsEntryPoint::class.java ) val analytics = entryPoint.getAnalytics()

Multibinding

Collect multiple implementations into a Set or Map — perfect for plugin architectures:

@Module @InstallIn(SingletonComponent::class) abstract class AnalyticsModule { @Binds @IntoSet abstract fun bindFirebase(impl: FirebaseTracker): AnalyticsTracker @Binds @IntoSet abstract fun bindMixpanel(impl: MixpanelTracker): AnalyticsTracker } // Inject the whole set: class AnalyticsManager @Inject constructor( private val trackers: Set<@JvmSuppressWildcards AnalyticsTracker> ) { fun track(event: Event) = trackers.forEach { it.track(event) } }

Testing with Hilt

@HiltAndroidTest @UninstallModules(RepositoryModule::class) class SearchScreenTest { @get:Rule val hiltRule = HiltAndroidRule(this) @BindValue val fakeRepo: UserRepository = FakeUserRepository() @Test fun showsResults() { ... } }
@UninstallModules removes the real module. @BindValue injects your fake directly. This lets you test with controlled data without mocking frameworks.

questions.title: Hilt Component Hierarchy
questions.description: In Hilt's component hierarchy, which component is the direct parent of ViewModelComponent?
questions.explanation: ViewModelComponent is a child of ActivityRetainedComponent. This means ViewModelScoped bindings can access ActivityRetainedScoped and Singleton bindings, but NOT ActivityScoped or FragmentScoped bindings. The hierarchy mirrors lifecycle: Singleton > ActivityRetained > ViewModel > Activity > Fragment.
hints: Think about what survives configuration changes
ViewModels survive rotation — their parent component must too

questions.title: @Binds Interface Binding
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to bind the UserRepositoryImpl to the UserRepository interface using Hilt.
questions.explanation: @Binds must be an abstract fun in an abstract class. It tells Hilt: whenever someone requests UserRepository, provide UserRepositoryImpl. This generates less code than @Provides and is preferred for simple interface-to-implementation mappings.
hints: @Binds functions have no body — they're abstract
The return type is the interface, the parameter is the implementation

questions.title: Wrong Scope — Data Lost on Rotation
questions.description: This app loses its cached search results every time the user rotates the device. The ViewModel survives rotation, but the repository doesn't retain its in-memory cache. Fix the scoping issue.

checks.message: Install in a component that survives configuration changes
Use a scope that survives rotation

questions.explanation: @ActivityScoped bindings are destroyed when the Activity is destroyed — including on rotation. Since repositories typically hold app-wide cached data, they should be @Singleton (in SingletonComponent) or at minimum @ActivityRetainedScoped (survives config changes). The general rule: scope data based on its lifetime, not the component that uses it.
hints: ActivityComponent is destroyed on every configuration change
What component survives rotation? What scope goes with it?

questions.title: Assisted Injection for ViewModel
questions.description: Write a ViewModel that uses assisted injection to receive a userId: String at runtime (from navigation arguments) while also getting a UserRepository from Hilt. Include the factory interface.
questions.explanation: @AssistedInject tells Dagger/Hilt this class has a mix of DI-provided and runtime parameters. @Assisted marks the runtime ones. The @AssistedFactory interface defines how to create instances with the runtime parameter. Hilt generates the implementation.
hints: @AssistedInject goes on the constructor, @Assisted on the runtime parameter
The factory interface has a single create() method returning the ViewModel

questions.title: @EntryPoint Access Pattern
questions.description: What does this code accomplish and when would you use this pattern?
options: It creates a new Hilt component specifically for the ContentProvider
It accesses Hilt-managed dependencies from a class that Hilt doesn't manage (ContentProvider)
It replaces the ImageLoader binding for testing purposes
It installs a new module into the SingletonComponent at runtime
questions.explanation: @EntryPoint provides an escape hatch for accessing Hilt-managed dependencies from classes that Hilt doesn't control. ContentProviders can't use @AndroidEntryPoint because they're initialized before the Application. EntryPointAccessors.fromApplication() reaches into the existing Hilt graph to get the dependency.
hints: ContentProviders are initialized before Hilt is set up
This is about accessing the DI graph from outside the normal injection flow

questions.title: @Binds vs @Provides
options: When the dependency is an interface
When the class has no constructor parameters
When the class is from a third-party library and you can't add @Inject to its constructor
When you want the dependency to be a singleton
questions.explanation: @Binds only works when Hilt can construct the implementation itself (the class has @Inject constructor). For third-party classes (Retrofit, OkHttp, Room), you can't modify their source to add @Inject, so you must use @Provides with a function body that constructs the instance. You also need @Provides when construction requires builder patterns or configuration.
hints: Think about what @Binds needs to work — it has no function body
Can you modify Retrofit's constructor to add @Inject?

questions.title: Multibinding with @IntoSet
questions.description: Create a Hilt module that collects multiple AnalyticsTracker implementations into a Set, and an AnalyticsManager class that injects and uses all of them.

checks.message: Use @IntoSet to contribute to the Set
Inject a Set of trackers in the manager

questions.explanation: @IntoSet contributes each binding to a Set. The AnalyticsManager injects the entire set and iterates over all trackers. @JvmSuppressWildcards is needed because Kotlin generics produce wildcards that Dagger can't match. This pattern is ideal for plugin architectures where you can add new implementations without changing existing code.
hints: Each @Binds @IntoSet contributes one element to the Set
You may need @JvmSuppressWildcards on the Set parameter

questions.title: Circular Dependency
questions.description: This code causes a Hilt compilation error due to a circular dependency: UserManager needs AuthService, and AuthService needs UserManager. Fix it using Lazy or Provider.

checks.message: Use Lazy<T> or Provider<T> to break the cycle

questions.explanation: Lazy<T> (from dagger.Lazy) defers creation until .get() is called, breaking the circular dependency. By the time logout() calls userManager.get(), the UserManager has already been fully constructed. Alternative: use Provider<T> which creates a new instance each time (vs Lazy which caches). Best fix: refactor to eliminate the cycle entirely.
hints: Lazy<T> delays construction — the object isn't created until .get() is called
Break the cycle on the side that doesn't need the dependency immediately

questions.title: Koin vs Hilt
questions.description: Which of the following is a key advantage of Hilt/Dagger over Koin?
options: Hilt is easier to set up with fewer annotations
Hilt validates the dependency graph at compile time, catching missing bindings before runtime
Hilt supports Kotlin Multiplatform (KMP) while Koin does not
Hilt uses less memory because it doesn't generate code
questions.explanation: Hilt/Dagger performs compile-time validation — if a dependency is missing, you get a build error, not a runtime crash. Koin is a service locator that resolves dependencies at runtime, so missing bindings cause NoBeanDefFoundException in production. However, Koin is simpler to set up, pure Kotlin, and supports KMP. Hilt generates more code but catches errors earlier.
hints: Think about when errors are discovered — at build time or at runtime
Which approach gives you more safety for production apps?

questions.title: Hilt Testing Setup
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to set up a Hilt test that replaces the real repository with a fake.

blanks.hint: Annotation that enables Hilt in tests
Injects this value into the Hilt graph

questions.explanation: @HiltAndroidTest enables Hilt in the test. @UninstallModules removes the real module so you can replace bindings. @BindValue adds your fake directly to the Hilt graph — simpler than creating a test module. hiltRule.inject() triggers field injection on the test class.
hints: The test annotation is specific to Hilt testing
@BindValue is the simplest way to override a binding in tests
